subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Think about the water your family drinks. If it comes from a well, a river like the Jubba, or a rain barrel, it is almost never truly clean. It carries bits of sand, mud, tiny living germs, and sometimes dissolved salt you cannot even see. Now think about the salt your mother sprinkles into a pot of bariis, or the sugar that sweetens a glass of shaah. Both of these were once locked inside something else - salt inside sea water, sugar inside a sugar-cane plant - and clever people used science to pull them out. In this unit you will learn the science of mixtures and how to separate them. It is one of the most practical, life-saving topics in all of chemistry.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Explain the difference between a pure substance and a mixture, and give everyday examples of each.
Define and use the words solution, solvent, solute and dissolve correctly.
Describe what solubility means and list the factors that make a solid dissolve faster.
Explain what a saturated solution is and how it forms.
Choose and describe the correct separation method - filtration, evaporation, crystallisation, distillation or chromatography - for a given mixture.
Explain how each separation method works using the different properties of the substances involved.
Apply these methods to real problems such as getting clean water from muddy or salty water.
Read and interpret a simple solubility result and a chromatography result.
Study this table carefully. These words are the tools of a chemist who works with mixtures. Notice how the example column ties each word to something you already know from home.

concepts.title: Pure Substances and Mixtures
concepts.explanation: The first big idea in this unit is the difference between something that is pure and something that is a mixture. In everyday speech, 'pure' can mean clean or natural. In science it has a much more exact meaning. A pure substance is made of only one type of particle, with nothing else mixed in. Pure water contains only water particles. Pure salt contains only salt particles. Pure sugar contains only sugar particles. Because a pure substance is always the same all the way through, it behaves in a fixed, predictable…
concepts.example: A mixture is made of two or more substances that are simply mixed together and not chemically joined. This is the key point: in a mixture, each substance keeps its own properties and can be separated out again. When you…

concepts.title: Solutions, Solvents and Solutes
concepts.explanation: One of the most important kinds of mixture is a solution. You make solutions every single day, probably without thinking about it. When your family makes shaah and stirs in sugar, the sugar seems to vanish. It has not disappeared - it has dissolved, and you have made a solution. Let us learn the proper words for what is going on. Whenever something dissolves, three words describe what is happening:
concepts.example: The solvent is the liquid that does the dissolving. In a glass of sweet shaah, the water is the solvent.

concepts.title: Solubility and Saturation
concepts.explanation: Now we go a step deeper. It is not enough to know whether something dissolves - a good scientist also asks how much dissolves, and how fast. This is the idea of solubility. Solubility tells us how much of a solute will dissolve in a certain amount of solvent at a given temperature. Some solutes are very soluble - you can dissolve a great deal of sugar in a cup of water. Others are only slightly soluble. Solubility is not a fixed, single number, because it depends strongly on the temperature of the solvent, as you…
concepts.example: If you have ever waited for sugar to dissolve in cold water, you know it can be slow. Three things speed up dissolving, and you can feel each of them in your own kitchen:

concepts.title: Separation Methods
concepts.explanation: Now we come to the heart of the unit: how to separate a mixture back into its parts. The golden rule is always the same - find a property that is different between the substances, and use that difference to separate them. A different particle size, whether something dissolves, a different boiling point, or how strongly a dye sticks to paper - each difference gives us a method. Let us take the five main methods in turn. Filtration separates an insoluble solid from a liquid. You pour the mixture through a filter -…
concepts.example: Filtration is one of the most useful skills in daily life. When your grandmother pours a pot of shaah through a strainer, the tea leaves (residue) stay behind and the clear tea (filtrate) flows into the cup. When a…

concepts.title: Choosing the Right Method
concepts.explanation: A real scientist does not just know the methods - they know which one to choose for a given mixture. This is where all your learning comes together. To choose correctly, ask yourself a short chain of questions about the mixture: Is it a solid mixed with a liquid? If the solid is insoluble (like sand or mud), use filtration. If the solid is dissolved (soluble), filtration will not work.
concepts.example: Do I want to keep the dissolved solid? If yes, use evaporation (for a quick dry solid) or crystallisation (for pure, well-formed crystals).

explorations.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtration
explorations.context: To use filtration to separate the mud and sand out of dirty water, and to see what filtration can and cannot do.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write: "I predict that after filtering, the water will look ... and the cloth will have ... left on it." Fill in the gaps with what you expect.
explorations.answer: What is the scientific name for the solid left on the cloth, and for the liquid that passed through? Did filtering remove ALL the dirt, or was the water still a little cloudy? Why might tiny bits still get through? Would filtering remove salt if the water were salty? Explain why or why not. Is the…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To use filtration to separate the mud and sand out of dirty water, and to see what filtration can and cannot do.

explorations.title: Getting Salt Back from Salt Water (Evaporation)
explorations.context: To use evaporation to separate dissolved salt from water, just as sea-salt farmers do along the coast.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "When the water evaporates I think the salt will ..."
explorations.answer: Where did the water go? It was not destroyed - what happened to it? Why was the salt left behind on the plate when the water was not? This is the exact method used to make sea salt on the coast. Why is a hot, sunny place perfect for it? If you wanted to keep the WATER instead of the salt, why would…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To use evaporation to separate dissolved salt from water, just as sea-salt farmers do along the coast.

explorations.title: Hidden Colours: Paper Chromatography
explorations.context: To use paper chromatography to separate a single ink or dye into the different colours hidden inside it.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think the black (or dark) pen will separate into ... colours."
explorations.answer: Did the single ink dot separate into more than one colour? What does this tell you about the ink? Why did some colours travel further up the paper than others? The colour that travelled furthest dissolves best and sticks least to the paper. Explain in your own words. Why must the ink dot start…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To use paper chromatography to separate a single ink or dye into the different colours hidden inside it.

explorations.title: The Dissolving and Saturation Test
explorations.context: To investigate how temperature, stirring and grain size affect dissolving, and to make a saturated solution.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think salt (or sugar) will dissolve fastest in ... water, and I think a cup of water can only hold about ... spoonfuls before no more will dissolve."
explorations.answer: In which cup did the salt dissolve fastest? What does this tell you about temperature and dissolving? Did stirring make the salt dissolve faster? Explain why stirring helps. What does it mean when no more salt will dissolve? What is this solution called? You made a saturated solution in the hot…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate how temperature, stirring and grain size affect dissolving, and to make a saturated solution.

explorations.title: Catching Pure Water (Simple Distillation)
explorations.context: To collect pure water from salty water by boiling it gently and catching the steam on a cool lid - a simple, safe model of distillation.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think the water that drips off the cool lid will be ... (salty or not salty), because ..."
explorations.answer: The water in the cup came from steam. Why was it NOT salty? Why did the salt stay behind in the pot instead of rising with the steam? Name the two changes of state that happened: one when the water left the pot, and one when it landed on the lid. Why is distillation, and not evaporation, the right…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To collect pure water from salty water by boiling it gently and catching the steam on a cool lid - a simple, safe model of distillation.

methods.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtration
methods.example: To use filtration to separate the mud and sand out of dirty water, and to see what filtration can and cannot do.
steps: Stir the soil into the water so you have a good muddy mixture. Look at it and note its colour.
Fold the cloth several times to make a thick filter, and lay it over the mouth of an empty clean cup.
Slowly pour the muddy water through the cloth into the cup. Do not let it overflow.
Look at the water that has passed through (the filtrate) and at the cloth (the residue).
If the water is still cloudy, pour it through a fresh part of the cloth a second time.

methods.title: Getting Salt Back from Salt Water (Evaporation)
methods.example: To use evaporation to separate dissolved salt from water, just as sea-salt farmers do along the coast.
steps: Dissolve two or three spoonfuls of salt in a small cup of warm water. Stir until the water looks clear and you cannot see the salt any more.
Talk about it: the salt is the solute, the water is the solvent, and the clear salty water is the solution.
Pour a thin layer of the salt solution onto the dark plate.
Put the plate in a warm, sunny place where it will not be disturbed.
Check it once or twice a day for two to four days as the water slowly disappears.

methods.title: Hidden Colours: Paper Chromatography
methods.example: To use paper chromatography to separate a single ink or dye into the different colours hidden inside it.
steps: Draw a thick dot of ink about 2 cm up from the bottom end of the paper strip. Press hard so the colour is strong.
Pour about 1 cm of water into the cup.
Hang the strip so its bottom edge just dips into the water, but the ink dot stays ABOVE the water line. Tape the top to a pencil laid across the cup.
Watch as the water slowly climbs up the paper and reaches the ink dot.
Leave it for 10 to 20 minutes as the water carries the colours up the paper.

methods.title: The Dissolving and Saturation Test
methods.example: To investigate how temperature, stirring and grain size affect dissolving, and to make a saturated solution.
steps: Part A - Temperature: Put one level spoonful of salt into each of the cold, warm and hot cups at the same time. Do NOT stir. Watch and time how long each takes to disappear.
Part B - Stirring: Add another spoonful to the cold cup and this time stir it. Does it dissolve faster than the one you did not stir?
Part C - Grain size (optional): Drop a sugar cube and the same amount of loose sugar into two cups of the same temperature. Which dissolves first?
Part D - Saturation: Take the hot-water cup. Keep adding salt one spoonful at a time, stirring after each, and counting the spoonfuls, until no more will dissolve and it collects at the bottom.
Record all your results in the table.

methods.title: Catching Pure Water (Simple Distillation)
methods.example: To collect pure water from salty water by boiling it gently and catching the steam on a cool lid - a simple, safe model of distillation.
steps: An adult pours the salty water into the pot to a shallow depth, and stands the empty clean cup in the middle so its rim is above the water.
Put the lid on the pot UPSIDE DOWN (so the dome points inwards, guiding drips towards the cup).
An adult heats the pot gently so the salty water simmers - not a violent boil.
Steam rises, touches the cool lid and condenses into drops that run down and drip into the clean cup.
After several minutes, the adult turns off the heat and lets everything cool before removing the cup.

workedExamples.title: Choosing and Explaining Methods
workedExamples.prompt: Follow the reasoning in each example. In every one, notice how we name the difference between the substances and use it to pick a method.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1 Question: Halima has a bowl of bariis (rice) with small stones mixed in. How should she separate them, and what property is she using? Step 1 - Find the difference. The stones and the rice grains are different sizes and colours, and both are solids that are easy to see. Step 2 - Choose the method. Because the difference is in size and appearance, she can separate them by hand - hand-sorting or sieving the smaller grains from the larger stones.

workedExamples.prompt: Question: Halima has a bowl of bariis (rice) with small stones mixed in. How should she separate them, and what property is she using?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Find the difference. The stones and the rice grains are different sizes and colours, and both are solids that are easy to see. Step 2 - Choose the method. Because the difference is in size and appearance, she can separate them by hand - hand-sorting or sieving the smaller grains from the larger stones. Answer: She hand-sorts or sieves them, using the difference in size between the stones and the rice. No dissolving is involved, so no filter or heat is needed. Worked Example 2

workedExamples.prompt: Question: Farah has salty water and wants to collect the salt. Which method should he use, and what happens to the water?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Find the difference. The salt is dissolved (soluble), so it cannot be filtered. But salt does not evaporate at the temperatures water does. Step 2 - Choose the method. Since he wants the solid and not the water, he uses evaporation: gently warm the salty water so the water turns to vapour and escapes. Answer: He uses evaporation. The water escapes into the air as water vapour, and the salt is left behind - exactly as sea-salt farmers do with sunshine. Worked Example 3

workedExamples.prompt: Question: Khadija has salty water but this time wants the pure water to drink, not the salt. Which method, and why is it different from Example 2?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Find the difference. Water boils and turns to steam at 100 degrees Celsius, but the salt stays behind because it does not turn to gas at that temperature. Step 2 - Choose the method. Because she wants to keep the water, she must catch the escaping steam and cool it back into a liquid - this is distillation. Answer: She uses distillation. She boils the salty water, and the steam condenses on a cool lid into pure water, leaving the salt behind. Unlike evaporation, distillation saves the water instead of…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is soluble in water? (a) sand (b) sugar (c) charcoal (d) cooking oil
workedExamples.solution: (b) sugar - salt and sugar are soluble; sand, charcoal and oil are not.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which makes a solid dissolve FASTER? (a) cooling the water (b) using bigger lumps (c) stirring (d) leaving it still
workedExamples.solution: (c) stirring - stirring, heating and crushing all speed up dissolving; cooling and big lumps slow it down.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A solution that can hold no more dissolved solute is called: (a) dilute (b) saturated (c) pure (d) insoluble
workedExamples.solution: (b) saturated - it is holding as much solute as it possibly can.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: To separate sand from water you would use: (a) distillation (b) chromatography (c) filtration (d) evaporation
workedExamples.solution: (c) filtration - sand is insoluble, so a filter catches it.

practice.prompt: A substance made of only one type of particle is called: (a) a mixture (b) a solution (c) a pure substance (d) a solvent
practice.answer: (c) a pure substance - it is made of only one type of particle.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In salty water, the salt is the: (a) solvent (b) solute (c) solution (d) filtrate
practice.answer: (b) solute - the salt is the substance that dissolves.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In salty water, the water is the: (a) solvent (b) solute (c) residue (d) crystal
practice.answer: (a) solvent - the water is the liquid that does the dissolving.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A substance that does NOT dissolve is described as: (a) soluble (b) saturated (c) insoluble (d) pure
practice.answer: (c) insoluble - it cannot dissolve, so it stays as solid bits.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is soluble in water? (a) sand (b) sugar (c) charcoal (d) cooking oil
practice.answer: (b) sugar - salt and sugar are soluble; sand, charcoal and oil are not.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which makes a solid dissolve FASTER? (a) cooling the water (b) using bigger lumps (c) stirring (d) leaving it still
practice.answer: (c) stirring - stirring, heating and crushing all speed up dissolving; cooling and big lumps slow it down.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A solution that can hold no more dissolved solute is called: (a) dilute (b) saturated (c) pure (d) insoluble
practice.answer: (b) saturated - it is holding as much solute as it possibly can.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To separate sand from water you would use: (a) distillation (b) chromatography (c) filtration (d) evaporation
practice.answer: (c) filtration - sand is insoluble, so a filter catches it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To get salt back from salty water you would use: (a) filtration (b) evaporation (c) chromatography (d) sieving
practice.answer: (b) evaporation - the water leaves as vapour and the salt is left behind.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The solid left on the filter paper is called the: (a) filtrate (b) solvent (c) residue (d) solution
practice.answer: (c) residue - the solid trapped on the filter is the residue; the liquid through it is the filtrate.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To get PURE WATER from salty water you would use: (a) filtration (b) evaporation (c) distillation (d) crystallisation
practice.answer: (c) distillation - it catches and cools the steam to give pure water back.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Separating the different colours in an ink is done by: (a) filtration (b) chromatography (c) distillation (d) evaporation
practice.answer: (b) chromatography - it separates different coloured dyes.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Cleaning Muddy Water by Filtration
activities.materials: A cup of clean water with a spoonful of soil, sand or mud stirred in.; A clean cotton cloth or an old (clean) T-shirt, folded several times - or a paper coffee/tea filter.; Two clear cups or jars.; A rubber band or string to hold the cloth over a cup (optional).
steps: Stir the soil into the water so you have a good muddy mixture. Look at it and note its colour.
Fold the cloth several times to make a thick filter, and lay it over the mouth of an empty clean cup.
Slowly pour the muddy water through the cloth into the cup. Do not let it overflow.
Look at the water that has passed through (the filtrate) and at the cloth (the residue).
If the water is still cloudy, pour it through a fresh part of the cloth a second time.

activities.title: Getting Salt Back from Salt Water (Evaporation)
activities.materials: A few spoonfuls of table salt.; Warm water.; A dark-coloured plate or a shallow dish (dark makes the white salt easier to see).; A sunny windowsill or a warm, safe place.; A spoon for stirring.
steps: Dissolve two or three spoonfuls of salt in a small cup of warm water. Stir until the water looks clear and you cannot see the salt any more.
Talk about it: the salt is the solute, the water is the solvent, and the clear salty water is the solution.
Pour a thin layer of the salt solution onto the dark plate.
Put the plate in a warm, sunny place where it will not be disturbed.
Check it once or twice a day for two to four days as the water slowly disappears.

activities.title: Hidden Colours: Paper Chromatography
activities.materials: A strip of white paper towel or a coffee/tea filter, cut about 3 cm wide and 12 cm long.; Water-based felt-tip pens or markers - a black one works best, plus any dark colours. (Avoid permanent markers, which do not dissolve in water.); A clear cup with a little water in the bottom (about 1 cm…
steps: Draw a thick dot of ink about 2 cm up from the bottom end of the paper strip. Press hard so the colour is strong.
Pour about 1 cm of water into the cup.
Hang the strip so its bottom edge just dips into the water, but the ink dot stays ABOVE the water line. Tape the top to a pencil laid across the cup.
Watch as the water slowly climbs up the paper and reaches the ink dot.
Leave it for 10 to 20 minutes as the water carries the colours up the paper.

activities.title: The Dissolving and Saturation Test
activities.materials: Table salt or sugar.; Three clear cups: one with cold water, one with warm water, one with hot water (an adult handles the hot water).; A spoon for stirring and a teaspoon for measuring.; Optional: a small lump of rock salt or sugar cube to compare with the fine grains.
steps: Part A - Temperature: Put one level spoonful of salt into each of the cold, warm and hot cups at the same time. Do NOT stir. Watch and time how long each takes to disappear.
Part B - Stirring: Add another spoonful to the cold cup and this time stir it. Does it dissolve faster than the one you did not stir?
Part C - Grain size (optional): Drop a sugar cube and the same amount of loose sugar into two cups of the same temperature. Which dissolves first?
Part D - Saturation: Take the hot-water cup. Keep adding salt one spoonful at a time, stirring after each, and counting the spoonfuls, until no more will dissolve and it collects at the bottom.
Record all your results in the table.

activities.title: Catching Pure Water (Simple Distillation)
activities.materials: A cooking pot with a lid (a domed lid works best).; Salty water (a spoonful of salt stirred into water).; A small clean heatproof cup or bowl that fits inside the pot.; An adult to manage the stove and the hot pot - this experiment uses heat.
steps: An adult pours the salty water into the pot to a shallow depth, and stands the empty clean cup in the middle so its rim is above the water.
Put the lid on the pot UPSIDE DOWN (so the dome points inwards, guiding drips towards the cup).
An adult heats the pot gently so the salty water simmers - not a violent boil.
Steam rises, touches the cool lid and condenses into drops that run down and drip into the clean cup.
After several minutes, the adult turns off the heat and lets everything cool before removing the cup.

terms: Pure substance
Made of only one type of particle; has a fixed melting and boiling point.
Mixture
Two or more substances mixed but not chemically joined; parts can be separated.
Solution
A mixture where a solute is fully dissolved in a solvent; looks clear.
Solvent
The liquid that does the dissolving (usually water).
Solute
The substance that dissolves into the solvent.
Dissolve
When a solute breaks up and spreads evenly through a solvent.
Soluble
Able to dissolve in a solvent.
Insoluble
Not able to dissolve; stays as solid bits.
Solubility
How much solute dissolves in a solvent at a given temperature.
Saturated solution
A solution holding as much dissolved solute as it possibly can.
Filtration
Separating an insoluble solid from a liquid using a filter.
Residue
The solid left behind on the filter.
commonMistakes: Thinking filtered water is safe to drink.
Thinking a clear liquid must be pure.

realProblems.prompt: During a drought near Garissa, Idris has only muddy well water. Describe, step by step, how he can make it clear AND safe to drink, naming each method.
realProblems.answer: 1. First filter the muddy water through a clean cloth to remove the mud and sand (the residue), giving clearer water. Then boil that filtered water for several minutes to kill the germs. Only after boiling is it safe to drink. (If the water were also salty, he would need to distil it.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. First filter the muddy water through a clean cloth to remove the mud and sand (the residue), giving clearer water. Then boil that filtered water for several minutes to kill the germs. Only after boiling is it safe to drink. (If the water were also salty, he would need to distil it.)

realProblems.prompt: Sea-salt farmers on the Indian Ocean coast let sea water dry in shallow pans in the sun. Name the method and explain why the salt is left behind while the water is not.
realProblems.answer: 2. This is evaporation. The sun's heat turns the water into water vapour, which escapes into the air, while the salt is left behind in the pan because salt does not evaporate at that temperature.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. This is evaporation. The sun's heat turns the water into water vapour, which escapes into the air, while the salt is left behind in the pan because salt does not evaporate at that temperature.

realProblems.prompt: Sahra wants pure drinking water from salty sea water. Explain why she must use distillation and not evaporation, and describe what happens to the steam.
realProblems.answer: 3. She must use distillation because she wants to keep the water, not the salt. She boils the salty water; the steam (pure water vapour) rises, leaving the salt behind, then condenses on a cool surface back into pure liquid water, which she collects. Evaporation would lose the water into the air.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. She must use distillation because she wants to keep the water, not the salt. She boils the salty water; the steam (pure water vapour) rises, leaving the salt behind, then condenses on a cool surface back into pure liquid water, which she collects. Evaporation would lose the water into the air.

realProblems.prompt: Halima's mother strains a pot of shaah before pouring it. Which separation method is this? What are the tea leaves and the clear tea called in scientific words?
realProblems.answer: 4. This is filtration. The tea leaves caught in the strainer are the residue, and the clear tea that passes through is the filtrate.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. This is filtration. The tea leaves caught in the strainer are the residue, and the clear tea that passes through is the filtrate.

realProblems.prompt: Omar picks the small stones out of a bowl of bariis before cooking. What property is he using to separate the mixture, and why can he not use filtration for this?
realProblems.answer: 5. He is using the difference in size (and appearance) between the stones and the rice grains, sorting them by hand or with a sieve. Filtration is for separating an insoluble solid from a liquid; here there is no liquid, just two solids, so filtration does not apply.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. He is using the difference in size (and appearance) between the stones and the rice grains, sorting them by hand or with a sieve. Filtration is for separating an insoluble solid from a liquid; here there is no liquid, just two solids, so filtration does not apply.

realProblems.prompt: A mixture contains sand, salt and water all together. Describe, in the correct order, how you would separate all three, and explain why the order matters.
realProblems.answer: 1. First filter the mixture: the insoluble sand is caught as residue, and salty water passes through as filtrate. Then evaporate (or distil) the salty water: if you evaporate it, the water leaves and the salt is left behind; if you distil it, you also collect the pure water. The order matters…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. First filter the mixture: the insoluble sand is caught as residue, and salty water passes through as filtrate. Then evaporate (or distil) the salty water: if you evaporate it, the water leaves and the salt is left behind; if you distil it, you also collect the pure water. The order matters…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a pure substance and a mixture.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. A pure substance is made of only one type of particle, with nothing mixed in (like pure water). A mixture is two or more substances mixed together but not chemically joined (like sea water), and its parts can be separated again.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Define the words solute, solvent and solution, using sweet shaah as your example.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. In sweet shaah, the solute is the sugar (the thing that dissolves), the solvent is the water (the liquid that dissolves it), and the solution is the sweet tea you get when the sugar has dissolved evenly.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between soluble and insoluble? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Soluble means able to dissolve (for example, salt or sugar in water). Insoluble means unable to dissolve (for example, sand or oil in water).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name three things you can do to make a solid dissolve faster.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Any three of: heat the solvent (warm water); stir the mixture; crush the solid into smaller pieces or powder.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is a saturated solution? What happens if you cool a hot saturated solution?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. A saturated solution is one holding as much dissolved solute as it possibly can. If you cool a hot saturated solution, it can no longer hold all the solute, so the extra solute comes out as solid crystals.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how filtration works, using the words residue and filtrate.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. In filtration you pour the mixture through filter paper or cloth. The insoluble solid is too big to pass through and is trapped as the residue; the liquid passes through as the filtrate.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can explain the difference between a pure substance and a mixture, and give everyday examples of each.
I can define and use the words solution, solvent, solute and dissolve correctly.
I can describe what solubility means and list the factors that make a solid dissolve faster.
I can explain what a saturated solution is and how it forms.
I can choose and describe the correct separation method - filtration, evaporation, crystallisation, distillation or chromatography - for a given mixture.
I can explain how each separation method works using the different properties of the substances involved.
I can apply these methods to real problems such as getting clean water from muddy or salty water.
